text: Al Gallodoro was born in Chicago on June 20, 1913. His virtuosity on the clarinet and saxophone has taken him from the vaudeville theaters of New Orleans to Carnegie Hall, and at age 92 he still enthralls audiences with his musicianship. He received an honorary degree from Hartwick College in September of 2005. Al was interviewed in Oneonta, New York on December 28, 2005. JoAnn Chmielowski, his pianist and manager, also participated in the interview, which was conducted by Monk Rowe, Director of the Fillius Jazz Archive.
doc_title: Al Gallodoro interviewed by Monk Rowe, Oneonta, New York, December 28, 2005

speaker: AG
text: You have it. Well we hired a 50 piece orchestra. We each put up $1,350. The session started right in Carnegie Hall at midnight. And we went until six in the morning. And I did the whole three movements of the “Concerto for Doubles.” Alto sax, bass clarinet, clarinet. Then I did “Oodles of Noodles” and the “Fantasy Impromptu.” But I was in my prime then. 50 piece orchestra. My brother Frank was in the band. And I went to the office one day and he gave me a check for about $240. He says, “I’m going to send Ralph his money” because Ralph went back to Milwaukee. That’s where he was from, Milwaukee, Wisconsin. But Ralph never received any money. They were scoundrels, I’ll tell you right now. They absconded with the money. They absconded with the original platter. Ralph could never get it back. Sam, Irving Berman was the other fellow. He was on the Unfair List for about two or three years in…

And you know, I want quiet when I drive. Yeah that’s going to happen. So I mean I would do a gig on the weekend and be like lifted. And then Monday morning, 6:30 a.m. there’s kids on the bus screaming and I’m going, “I just did this and now I’m doing this.” So it brings back some reality. We were able to play concert venues, Carnegie Hall where we were also able to go into, the south side of Chicago and play The Sutherland. So you could do so many more things then than you can today. text: In the tradition of the multi-instrumentalists of the small jazz bands of the 1920s, Scott Robinson excels on multiple brass and reed horns. He has found a special niche in the jazz world today on the C melody, bass and baritone saxophones, as well as maintaining a reputation as a fine soloist and ensemble player on the more familiar brass and reed instruments. He shares his home with over two hundred horns and is always searching for rare additions for the collection. Scott has recorded with many jazz greats, including Frank Wess, Louie Bellson and Toshiko Akiyoshi. Recently he has recorded as a leader for Arbors Records where he chose to feature the rare and imposing contrabass saxophone on his release “Thinking Big.” A constant traveler, he has appeared at festivals in Europe, Japan and the Caribbean as well as jazz events at the Smithsonian and Carnegie Hall.
doc_title: Scott Robinson interviewed by Monk Rowe,Chautauqua, New York, September 13, 1997

text: Cornetist Warren Vache is the first major player since Ruby Braff to be inspired by the work of Louis Armstrong. Born in Rahway, NJ, in 1951, he first gained acclaim at a Carnegie Hall concert where he recreated the solos of Bix Beiderbecke. Through numerous recordings on Concord Records, he has kept alive and added to the trumpet traditions established by Louis Armstrong, Ruby Braff, and Clifford Brown. He worked frequently with Benny Goodman and Woody Herman and is a familiar figure in jazz events around the country, often in the company of tenor saxist Scott Hamilton.

text: My name is John Mitchell. I’m a staff writer for the Los Angeles Times. Today is December 10th, 2006. We’re in the home of John Levy and his wife Devra Hall at 1827 Coolidge Avenue, Altadena, California. We’re here as part of the Smithsonian’s Jazz Oral History Project to record John Levy’s story. A former bass player, John has been managing the careers of music artists since 1951. In his musical prime he played the bass when jazz was at its heyday on New York’s 52nd Street. Duke Ellington wanted him in his orchestra. Billie Holiday hired him for her comeback concert at Carnegie Hall in 1948. Then John put away his upright to sit behind a desk and manage the careers of artists. He had a rich stable of clients: George Shearing, Dakota Staton, Betty Carter, Lou Donaldson, Herbie Mann, Les McCann, Ernie Andrews, Randy Crawford, Brook Benton, Ahmad Jamal, Freddie Hubbard, Cannonball…

text: He was not my composition teacher. He’s the only teacher I ever had in the creating of music, but it was limited just to theory and the rules of behavior in tonal music. At the same time, when I was 11 – well, 12 – when I was doing my homework, or on weekends, when I came home – we lived in a dormitory on 55th Street, right near Carnegie Hall, but on weekends we’d come home for one day. All the time that I had access to a radio, even when I was doing my homework later when I went back into high school after my voice broke, I was listening to jazz. I listened also to WQXR, which was the major classical station, and WNYC, but I listened to a lot of jazz. I was immediately drawn to it. In Germany I had heard no jazz at all. We were totally isolated, and anyway, Hitler had forbidden jazz. So there was no access.
